solution: Evaluate the inner function first: \( g([[x0]]) = ([[c]])([[x0]]) + ([[d]]) \). Then apply \( f \): \( f(g([[x0]])) = ([[a]])\big(g([[x0]])\big) + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( g(f([[x0]])) \), applying the functions in the reverse order.
This adds \( f([[x0]]) \) and \( g([[x0]]) \) instead of composing them.
This multiplies \( f([[x0]]) \) and \( g([[x0]]) \) instead of composing them.
This stops after the inner function and reports \( g([[x0]]) \).
This applies only the outer function to \( [[x0]] \), ignoring \( g \).
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Evaluate the inner function first: \( g([[x0]]) = ([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[b]]) \). Then apply \( f \): \( f(g([[x0]])) = \dfrac{1}{g([[x0]]) + [[c]]} = [[answer]] \).

solution: Evaluate the inner function first: \( g([[x0]]) = ([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[b]]) \). Then apply \( f \): \( f(g([[x0]])) = |g([[x0]])| + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the absolute value bars entirely, keeping the raw (possibly negative) value of \( g([[x0]]) \).
This negates the absolute value instead of leaving it positive.
This computes the absolute value correctly but forgets to add \( ([[c]]) \).
This takes the absolute value after adding \( [[c]] \) instead of before, changing the sign contribution of \( [[c]] \).
